specialist_findings.cardiologist: Reviewed 500 cardiac cases. Most relevant: PMC11462448 (coronary vasospasm causing refractory cardiogenic shock post-procedure), PMC4980526 (DRESS syndrome with refractory hypotension and cardiac involvement), PMC9293014 (constrictive pericarditis with tamponade physiology), PMC9248042 (cardiac tamponade from lymphoma - NO pericardial effusion visible on initial echo), PMC3872395 (primary cardiac lymphoma with tamponade), PMC3884349 (constrictive pericarditis after catheter ablation - similar post-procedure presentation). The index case's combination of post-PCI setting, severe refractory hypotension (60/40), IABP requirement, hemoglobin drop (13.2→11), and absence of pericardial effusion strongly suggests either: 1) coronary perforation with contained rupture/tamponade physiology without free effusion, 2) procedure-related constrictive pericarditis, or 3) systemic inflammatory response (DRESS) causing distributive shock with cardiac involvement.
specialist_findings.vascular_specialist: Reviewed 500 vascular cases. Most relevant: PMC4980526 (Refractory Hypotension from bilateral subclavian artery stenosis - illustrates how vascular pathology can cause refractory hypotension without obvious bleeding), PMC3874091 (retroperitoneal hemorrhage from endoleak - occult bleeding), PMC10519875 (cardiac tamponade), PMC9248042 (tamponade from lymphoma). The index case's severe hypotension refractory to fluids and vasopressors with hemoglobin drop but no visible effusion suggests either occult retroperitoneal/access site bleeding, coronary artery perforation with contained tamponade, or a vascular catastrophe like spontaneous coronary dissection with compression. The IABP was placed for hemodynamic support, suggesting primarily cardiac rather than peripheral vascular etiology.
specialist_findings.systemic_specialist: Reviewed 500 systemic cases. Most relevant: PMC4980526 (DRESS syndrome - drug reaction causing refractory hypotension and systemic inflammation), PMC320540 (hemophagocytic syndrome with shock), PMC1118741 (hemophagocytic syndrome with multi-organ failure), PMC3819886 (TTP/HUS with thrombocytopenia), PMC5825822 (systemic capillary leak syndrome causing refractory hypotension), PMC7758140 (hemorrhagic shock from occult tumor bleeding). The hemoglobin drop from 13.2 to 11 g/dL indicates blood loss, but no effusion on echo suggests the blood may be: 1) tracking into mediastinum/retrosternal space, 2) causing loculated pericardial compression not visible on standard views, or 3) lost via occult retroperitoneal bleed from access site. The refractory nature despite IABP suggests ongoing active process rather than simple vasovagal or drug effect.

pericardial_tamponade.reasoning: Multiple cardiac cases demonstrate tamponade physiology without visible pericardial effusion (PMC9248042, PMC9293014). Post-PCI setting makes coronary perforation with contained rupture/tamponade a strong consideration. However, echo explicitly states 'no pericardial effusion,' making classic tamponade less likely unless loculated or retro-sternal.

drug_induced_hypotension.reasoning: Systemic cases (PMC4980526 DRESS syndrome) demonstrate severe refractory hypotension from drug reactions. Cardiac case PMC4980526 shows similar pattern. However, index case lacks rash, fever, or other systemic allergic features. Contrast reaction possible but less likely given timing and presentation.

primary_diagnosis.reasoning: Most consistent with index case: Post-PCI setting, severe refractory hypotension despite IABP, hemoglobin drop (13.2→11), elevated venous pressures suggesting impaired cardiac filling, but NO visible pericardial effusion on echo. This pattern strongly suggests coronary artery perforation with blood tracking into myocardial tissue or loculated pericardial hematoma causing extrinsic compression (tamponade physiology without free effusion). Cardiac cases (PMC9248042, PMC3872395) demonstrate this exact phenomenon - tamponade without visible effusion.

most_discriminating_case.title: Primary cardiac lymphoma presenting with cardiac tamponade and complete heart block
most_discriminating_case.why: This case most closely mirrors the index case's key discriminating features: 1) Cardiac tamponade physiology with severe refractory hypotension, 2) Complete absence of visible pericardial effusion on initial echocardiography, 3) Requirement for hemodynamic support, 4) Elevated venous pressures suggesting impaired cardiac filling. The index case's post-PCI setting makes coronary perforation with contained/loculated hematoma or myocardial dissection with blood tracking into tissue the most likely diagnosis. This case proves that severe tamponade physiology can exist without visible pericardial effusion, teaching us to consider loculated pericardial hematoma or coronary perforation with contained rupture when echo is negative but clinical picture suggests tamponade.

weakest_domain_reasoning: While vascular cases demonstrated refractory hypotension (PMC4980526 - subclavian stenosis, PMC3874091 - retroperitoneal bleed), the index case's specific combination of post-PCI setting, elevated venous pressures suggesting right heart compression, and absence of pericardial effusion points primarily to a cardiac/pericardial etiology rather than peripheral vascular cause. The IABP placement indicates primary cardiac dysfunction rather than vascular etiology. Vascular domain provided fewer directly applicable cases for this specific constellation of findings compared to cardiac domain (tamponade without effusion) and systemic domain (refractory shock patterns).
synthesis: The index case presents a challenging post-PCI scenario: 75-year-old female, BP 60/40 refractory to fluids/vasopressors, IABP placed for support, hemoglobin drop 13.2→11 suggesting blood loss, yet NO pericardial effusion on echo. The most likely diagnosis is CORONARY ARTERY PERFORATION WITH CONTAINED RUPTURE OR LOCULATED PERICARDIAL HEMATOMA causing extrinsic cardiac compression and tamponade physiology without free pericardial effusion. Alternative considerations include: 1) Procedure-related constrictive pericarditis (PMC9293014, PMC3884349), 2) DRESS syndrome from contrast/media reaction (PMC4980526), 3) Retroperitoneal/access site bleeding causing hemorrhagic shock (PMC3874091). The key teaching point from this case review is that tamponade physiology can exist WITHOUT visible pericardial effusion - cases like PMC9248042 demonstrate cardiac compression from intramyocardial/ loculated processes that standard echo views miss. Urgent CT or MRI would be diagnostic.
